summary: Martin Fowler shares a writing tip from Bruce Eckel: read your writing out loud to catch awkward passages. The technique works because it engages the part of your brain that processes spoken language, which is more sensitive to bland or flabby prose. Fowler advocates for a conversational writing style, imagining he's explaining concepts to companions in a pub. He warns against corporate and academic prose styles, which he finds devoid of life and engagement. The goal is writing that readers can hear in the author's voice.
key_points: Reading your draft aloud (or at least moving your lips) engages your brain's spoken-word processing, which detects blandness better than silent reading
Conversational tone makes prose more engaging - aim for writing that sounds like you're explaining something to friends
Corporate prose and academic prose are the two main types of 'flab' that drain life from writing
The passive voice and bland corporate style are learned subconsciously and should be actively avoided
The ultimate test of good writing is whether readers can hear the author's voice when reading
money_quotes: By doing this you'll find bits that don't sound right, and need to fix.
I imagine I'm sitting in pub, explaining the concepts to my companions.
I've heard people say that when reading my work, they can hear me speaking it - which is exactly the effect I'm after.
Too often I read prose that feels flabby.
If they read their prose and it sounds like it could have come from Accenture, then they are doing it wrong.
And, of course, the passive voice is rarely preferred.
There is a perceptible corporate way of writing, often learned subconsciously, that is rife and ruinous.
Some sparkling prose by a colleague of mine was turned by editors at Microsoft into a tasteless pudding.
key_insight: Reading your writing aloud activates your brain's sensitivity to bland, lifeless prose in a way that silent reading cannot.
